acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run yzx dev bump with an explicit version and the command updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu to that version; the command creates a dedicated git commit for the version bump with a predictable message; the command creates the matching git tag after the commit succeeds; the command refuses to run when the git worktree is dirty; the command refuses to run when the requested tag already exists or conflicts with repository state; the command verifies that YAZELIX_VERSION matches the tag it creates; the command has automated tests covering the happy path and failure cases.

title: Rust rewrite with spec-driven feature development
description: Create a long-term plan for moving Yazelix from its current shell-script-heavy implementation toward a Rust core, while also making new feature work spec-driven by default. The main goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts.
design: Treat this as an architecture-and-process initiative, not just a language swap. The rewrite should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nix/devenv-driven, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. A key decision inside this epic is repository structure: explicitly evaluate whether the rewrite should happen inside the current Yazelix repo or start in a new repo, and document the tradeoffs around history continuity, migration cost, contributor clarity, and release strategy before committing to either path.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for a Rust-based Yazelix core, a defined spec template/workflow for feature development, and a documented decision on whether the rewrite lives in the current repo or a new repo. The plan identifies the first migration slices (for example launch, restart, config parsing, layout generation, workspace state), the test strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust, Nix/devenv, and any remaining shell glue. Leave behind enough architecture notes, specs, and migration steps that the rewrite can start incrementally instead of as a vague big-bang idea.

title: Fix Alt-( and Alt-) keybindings in Helix under Zellij
description: Fix the Alt-( and Alt-) Helix keybindings when running under Zellij so these editor shortcuts behave reliably instead of getting lost or mangled by the terminal and Zellij layer.
design: Treat this as an input-stack bug spanning terminal, Zellij, and Helix. First confirm what escape sequences are emitted in the supported terminal set, then decide whether the fix belongs in Zellij keybindings, terminal keyboard protocol settings, Helix mappings, or some narrower compatibility layer. Avoid a fix that only works in one terminal if Yazelix claims broader support.
acceptance_criteria: The affected Alt bindings work in the supported Yazelix terminal stack or the docs state the exact caveat when they cannot. Add unit tests for escape-sequence normalization or mapping helpers where they exist, regression tests or scripted input capture, and end-to-end validation with detailed logs of the emitted sequences and the final behavior inside Helix.

title: Make adding custom Zellij layouts straightforward
description: Imported GitHub issue #438 after verifying that custom Zellij layouts are only easy in a narrow no-sidebar case and are otherwise blocked by hardcoded layout generation, hardcoded sidebar-family knowledge in the pane orchestrator, and stale docs.
design: Treat this as a structural customization problem rather than a docs-only paper cut. The supported path for adding layouts should be explicit: layout files must be discoverable or registered through one clear mechanism, sidebar layout families must expose enough metadata for the pane orchestrator to switch and toggle them without hardcoded family names, and unsupported layouts must fail fast with clear errors instead of silently disappearing. The documentation should reflect the actual current filenames and the real extension workflow.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can add a supported custom layout without touching multiple unrelated code paths by hand; generated Zellij config picks up the new layout through the documented mechanism; sidebar-aware layouts either work through a documented manifest or naming scheme or fail fast with a clear error; docs describe the current layout files and the actual extension workflow; automated tests cover the supported custom-layout path.
close_reason: Made top-level Zellij layout discovery automatic, documented the supported customization boundary, and added regression coverage for custom layout generation.

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack (Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and a minimal set of supporting tools) plus the main command paths that currently depend on devenv. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.

title: Evaluate integrating sahaj-b/ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix-managed cursor effects
description: Evaluate adding curated support for the MIT-licensed ghostty-cursor-shaders repo to Yazelix so Ghostty cursor effects remain fully Yazelix-managed instead of requiring users to clone and wire shader files manually. The goal is to extend the current Ghostty cursor-trail system with upstream trail and pulse/boom patterns while preserving Yazelix generation, docs, defaults, and random selection.
design: Current state: Yazelix already generates Ghostty shader config, vendors local shader files under configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders, and defaults terminal.cursor_trail to random. The upstream repo is a good fit because Ghostty supports multiple custom-shader lines and the repo is simple standalone GLSL under MIT. Important caveat: these upstream effects are not only color variants. They include trail shaders like cursor_warp, cursor_sweep, and cursor_tail, plus mode-change shaders like ripple_cursor, sonic_boom_cursor, rectangle_boom_cursor, and ripple_rectangle_cursor. That means this should be treated as a broader cursor-effects surface, not just more colors. Preferred direction: keep Yazelix as the builder and orchestrator, vendor or generate a curated subset of upstream shaders locally, and expose them through a structured selector that supports both existing color presets and new pattern effects. A robust design is to support an ordered list for Ghostty shader selection and composition, with backward-compatible string aliases for existing cursor_trail values. If random remains the default, define a curated random pool explicitly instead of sampling every shader, because pulse and boom effects are behaviorally different from trails and may need separate inclusion rules. Also account for Ghostty-specific caveats such as custom-shader-animation = always for some line-cursor effects, and avoid regressing Kitty fallback behavior that currently maps random to snow.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear implementation decision and scope for integrating ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix. The chosen design keeps shader management inside Yazelix, documents the upstream source and license, and specifies whether the config surface stays terminal.cursor_trail, becomes truly list-capable, or is renamed to a broader cursor-effects concept. The plan identifies which upstream shaders are included, how random selection works, what Ghostty-only caveats must be emitted in generated config, and how existing cursor_trail users remain backward-compatible. Verification includes generating Ghostty config with a fixed preset, a composed or list-based preset if adopted, and the default random path.
close_reason: Implemented Ghostty cursor effect integration with vendored shaders, new Ghostty effect config, generated shader build, and collection/docs updates

title: Adopt minimal prek config for fast maintainer checks
description: Follow-up to the maintainer-pack work: clean up stale prek references that still describe an unrelated tool, add a minimal pre-commit-compatible config at the repo root, and keep the hook set intentionally small and cheap: syntax validation plus README version validation. Document the maintainer usage path without turning prek into the main enforcement layer; CI remains authoritative.
close_reason: Cleaned stale prek references, added a minimal .pre-commit-config.yaml with syntax and README version checks, documented the maintainer usage path, and verified the config with prek run --all-files.

title: replace welcome art with logo. make it work on half screens
description: Imported open GitHub issue #263.
close_reason: Covered by the broader welcome-screen redesign bead yazelix-2k9

title: Add Ghostty cursor effects for Zellij pane changes
description: Explore extending the Ghostty cursor-effect system beyond editor mode changes so Yazelix can trigger visual feedback on Zellij pane focus changes, such as ripple/boom effects when switching panes. Cover feasibility, event plumbing, and whether Ghostty shader triggers can be driven cleanly from Zellij/Yazelix without breaking existing cursor behavior.

title: Expand user config control where the merge cost is worth it
description: This is about giving users more control over generated tool configs without turning Yazelix into a fully generic config engine. The clearest remaining gap is terminal config merging: users should be able to layer more of their own terminal-emulator config on top of Yazelix-managed config when the merge model is safe and maintainable.
design: Treat this as an evaluation-first feature, not a promise to toml-ize every config surface. Start by identifying the low-hanging configuration areas where merging is practical and the maintenance cost is acceptable. The current likely target is terminal config merging. Keep the broader idea of a more fully controlled profile as direction, but do not expand this bead to cover `devenv` config control; that should stay in a separate bead. The bar is whether the extra user control is worth the implementation and support complexity.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and scope for which config surfaces should support richer user merging. If terminal config merging is worth it, Yazelix should support it in a maintainable way. If not, the bead should still produce a documented rationale and narrower control model rather than vague ambition about controlling everything. Any implementation path should include unit tests for merge semantics, end-to-end generation checks for the affected config surfaces, and detailed logs showing the user input, managed defaults, and final merged output.

title: Fix skip_welcome_screen=false being ignored during interactive launch
description: The core.skip_welcome_screen setting is currently being ignored for interactive Yazelix launch flows. Users with skip_welcome_screen = false still do not see the welcome UI.
design: Trace so far: the interactive launch path still calls show_welcome from nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, so the setting is not being ignored because the UI code was removed. The likely regression is environment leakage: launch wrappers pass --skip-welcome into the devenv/bootstrap layer, which sets YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME=true, and show_welcome treats that as test_mode. The most suspicious paths are nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu. The likely fix is to keep bootstrap welcome suppression for environment setup while clearing or scoping YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME before the real interactive inner session starts.
acceptance_criteria: With core.skip_welcome_screen = false, interactive Yazelix launch shows the welcome screen again. With core.skip_welcome_screen = true, the welcome screen is skipped and the info is logged as before. The fix must not regress env-only, one-shot command, or sweep/test flows that intentionally use YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME.

title: Auto-assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer
description: Automatically assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer instead of leaving assignment as a manual follow-up step.
design: Treat this as repository workflow automation, not product runtime work. Decide whether GitHub Actions, issue templates, or another automation path is the right place to assign newly opened issues to the maintainer, and verify the permission model before committing to an approach. The solution should be reliable without creating noisy side effects on transferred, reopened, or bot-created issues unless that behavior is explicitly desired.
acceptance_criteria: New issues are assigned to the intended maintainer automatically, or the repo has a documented explanation of why that automation is not supportable. Include validation for the chosen workflow path, with logs or dry-run evidence showing the assignment behavior for representative issue-opening scenarios.

title: Add CI check for README version drift
description: Protect the public README version marker from drifting away from YAZELIX_VERSION. Add one focused check that compares the README title/version line against the version constant and run it in CI.
close_reason: Completed: added README version drift validator to CI and taught yzx dev update to auto-sync the README title/version marker from YAZELIX_VERSION

title: Add maintainer pack for local maintainer tooling
description: Imported GitHub issue #453 after deciding that maintainer tooling should be an opt-in pack rather than a special yzx dev mode. Add a top-level maintainer pack for repo maintenance tools that are meaningfully tied to maintaining Yazelix itself, starting with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer. Keep runtime behavior unchanged, sync the default config and Home Manager module, document the pack in the collection/docs, and add a focused regression test that verifies the pack stays aligned across configuration paths.
close_reason: Added an opt-in maintainer pack with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer; synced the default config, Home Manager module, and collection docs; and added a regression test that keeps the pack aligned across both configuration paths.
